entities.name: LiveLoggingSystem
observations: [LLM] The LiveLoggingSystem component utilizes a modular design, with separate modules for session windowing, file routing, and classification layers. This is evident in the 'session_windowing.py' and 'file_routing.py' files, which contain functions such as 'window_session' and 'route_file' that handle these specific tasks. The 'classification_layers.py' file contains classes such as 'Classifier' that handle the classification of logs.

[LLM] The LiveLoggingSystem handles transcript capture through the 'transcript_capture.py' file, which contains a 'TranscriptCapture' class with methods such as 'capture_transcript' that utilize the 'session_windowing' module to capture transcripts from live sessions. The 'transcript_capture.py' file also imports the 'classification_layers' module to classify the captured transcripts.

[LLM] The component's use of the 'LSL' alias is reflected in the 'lsl_config.py' file, which contains configuration settings for the LiveLoggingSystem. The 'lsl_config.py' file is imported in the 'live_logging_system.py' file, which contains the main 'LiveLoggingSystem' class that orchestrates the live logging functionality. The 'live_logging_system.py' file also imports the 'specstory' module, which is used for handling SpecStory-related functionality.

[LLM] The LiveLoggingSystem's session windowing functionality is implemented using a sliding window approach, as seen in the 'window_session' function in the 'session_windowing.py' file. This function takes in a session object and a window size, and returns a list of windowed session data. The 'window_session' function is used in the 'live_logging_system.py' file to window live sessions before passing them to the 'transcript_capture' module for transcript capture.

[LLM] The classification layers in the LiveLoggingSystem are implemented using a layered approach, with each layer building on top of the previous one. This is evident in the 'classification_layers.py' file, which contains classes such as 'Classifier' and 'LayeredClassifier' that handle the classification of logs. The 'LayeredClassifier' class uses a list of 'Classifier' objects to classify logs in a layered manner, allowing for more accurate classification of logs.

[LLM] The LiveLoggingSystem's file routing functionality is implemented using a routing table approach, as seen in the 'route_file' function in the 'file_routing.py' file. This function takes in a file object and a routing table, and returns the routed file path. The 'route_file' function is used in the 'live_logging_system.py' file to route files to their respective destinations after transcript capture and classification.

[LLM] The LiveLoggingSystem's use of the 'specstory' module is reflected in the 'specstory_handler.py' file, which contains a 'SpecStoryHandler' class that handles SpecStory-related functionality. The 'SpecStoryHandler' class is used in the 'live_logging_system.py' file to handle SpecStory-related tasks, such as loading SpecStory configurations and handling SpecStory events.

entities.name: KnowledgeManagement
observations: [LLM] The KnowledgeManagement component utilizes a modular architecture, with separate modules for graph database adaptation, persistence, and semantic analysis. This is evident in the way the GraphDatabaseAdapter (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) is used for persistence, and the PersistenceAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/persistence-agent.ts) is used for managing entity persistence and ontology classification. The CodeGraphAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/code-graph-agent.ts) is also used for constructing code knowledge graphs and providing semantic code search capabilities. The ukb-trace-report (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/utils/ukb-trace-report.ts) is used for generating detailed trace reports of UKB workflow runs. This modular design allows for flexibility and maintainability of the component.

[LLM] The use of intelligent routing for API or direct access to the graph database is a key feature of the KnowledgeManagement component. This allows for flexible and efficient data management, and is implemented using the GraphDatabaseAdapter (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/storage/graph-database-adapter.ts). The adapter provides a standardized interface for interacting with the graph database, regardless of whether it is accessed through the API or directly. This design decision enables the component to handle large amounts of data and provide efficient querying capabilities.

[LLM] The KnowledgeManagement component employs a Graphology and LevelDB for graph storage, with automatic JSON export sync. The GraphDatabaseAdapter (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) is responsible for managing the storage and retrieval of data from the graph database. The use of LevelDB provides a high-performance and scalable storage solution, while Graphology provides a flexible and efficient way to manage the graph structure. The automatic JSON export sync feature ensures that the data is always up-to-date and consistent across the system.

[LLM] The scripts/migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js script is used for migrating entity types in the live LevelDB/Graphology database. This script is an important tool for maintaining the integrity and consistency of the data in the graph database. By using this script, developers can easily update the entity types in the database, without having to manually edit the data. This is particularly useful when working with large datasets, where manual editing would be time-consuming and prone to errors.

[LLM] The PersistenceAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/persistence-agent.ts) plays a crucial role in managing entity persistence and ontology classification. The agent is responsible for ensuring that the data is properly stored and retrieved from the graph database, and that the ontology classification is accurate and up-to-date. The agent uses the GraphDatabaseAdapter (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) to interact with the graph database, and provides a standardized interface for managing entity persistence and ontology classification.

[LLM] The CodeGraphAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/code-graph-agent.ts) is responsible for constructing code knowledge graphs and providing semantic code search capabilities. The agent uses the GraphDatabaseAdapter (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) to interact with the graph database, and provides a standardized interface for constructing and searching code knowledge graphs. The agent is an important component of the KnowledgeManagement system, as it enables developers to efficiently search and navigate the codebase.

[LLM] The ukb-trace-report (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/utils/ukb-trace-report.ts) is used for generating detailed trace reports of UKB workflow runs. The report provides valuable insights into the execution of the workflow, including information about the entities and relationships involved, and any errors or exceptions that occurred. The report is generated using data from the graph database, and is an important tool for debugging and optimizing the workflow.

[LLM] The CodingPatterns component employs a modular architecture, as evident in lib/llm/provider-registry.js, where different providers are registered and managed. This design enables extensibility and flexibility in the component, allowing new providers to be added or removed without affecting the overall system. Additionally, the consistent naming convention used throughout the project, such as PascalCase, enhances code readability and maintainability.

[LLM] The implementation of work-stealing concurrency in wave-controller.ts significantly improves the component's performance and efficiency. By leveraging this concurrency model, tasks are distributed among available worker threads, minimizing idle time and maximizing throughput. The 'WaveController' class in wave-controller.ts orchestrates the work-stealing process, utilizing methods like 'submitTask' and 'getTaskResult' to manage task execution and retrieval of results. This design enables the component to scale and handle large workloads, making it suitable for demanding applications.

[LLM] The CodingPatterns component integrates with the Specstory extension using the SpecstoryAdapter (lib/integrations/specstory-adapter.js), promoting decoupling and flexibility in the system. The SpecstoryAdapter provides a standardized interface for interacting with the Specstory extension, allowing the component to connect and retrieve data without being tightly coupled to the extension's implementation. The 'SpecstoryAdapter' class in specstory-adapter.js offers methods like 'connect' and 'disconnect' to manage the connection, as well as 'getData' to retrieve data from the extension. This adapter-based approach enables easy switching between different extensions or integrations if required.

[LLM] The LLMService class (lib/llm/llm-service.ts) plays a crucial role in the CodingPatterns component, providing language model-based analysis and mode routing capabilities. The 'LLMService' class utilizes caching to optimize performance, reducing the overhead of repeated computations and improving response times. The 'analyze' method in llm-service.ts serves as the primary entry point for language model-based analysis, while the 'getMode' method determines the appropriate mode for a given input. The caching mechanism, implemented using the 'CacheManager' class, ensures that frequently accessed data is stored in memory, minimizing the need for redundant computations and enhancing the overall efficiency of the component.

[LLM] The CodingPatterns component adheres to a consistent naming convention, such as PascalCase, throughout the project, enhancing code readability and maintainability. This convention is applied to class names, method names, and variable names, ensuring that the codebase is uniform and easy to navigate. For example, the 'GraphDatabaseAdapter' class in storage/graph-database-adapter.ts follows this convention, as do the 'ProviderRegistry' class in lib/llm/provider-registry.js and the 'WaveController' class in wave-controller.ts. This consistent naming scheme facilitates developer collaboration and reduces the learning curve for new team members, making it easier to understand and contribute to the codebase.

observations: [LLM] The ConstraintSystem component utilizes a modular architecture, with each module responsible for a specific aspect of constraint management. For instance, the ContentValidationAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/content-validation-agent.ts) is used for entity content validation against configured rules. This modular design allows for easy maintenance and scalability of the system. The HookConfigLoader (lib/agent-api/hooks/hook-config.js) is another example of this modularity, as it is responsible for loading and merging hook configurations from multiple sources. This separation of concerns enables developers to focus on specific aspects of the system without affecting other parts.

[LLM] The system's use of the GraphDatabaseAdapter (storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) for persistence is a key aspect of its architecture. This adapter enables the system to store data in a graph database, which is well-suited for complex, interconnected data. The GraphDatabaseAdapter also provides automatic JSON export sync, which ensures that the data is always up-to-date and easily accessible. The ViolationCaptureService (scripts/violation-capture-service.js) is another important component, as it is responsible for capturing constraint violations from tool interactions. This service works in conjunction with the GraphDatabaseAdapter to store and manage violation data.

[LLM] The ConstraintSystem component employs a work-stealing concurrency pattern, which is implemented using a shared atomic index counter in the runWithConcurrency() function (wave-controller.ts:489). This pattern allows the system to efficiently utilize multiple cores and process large amounts of data in parallel. The use of lazy initialization for the Large Language Model (LLM) in the ensureLLMInitialized() function (wave-controller.ts:234) is another important design decision. This approach ensures that the LLM is only initialized when it is actually needed, which can help to reduce memory usage and improve system performance.

The HookConfigLoader (lib/agent-api/hooks/hook-config.js) works in conjunction with the hook manager to load and merge hook configurations from multiple sources. This allows developers to easily configure hooks for different agents and scenarios. The ContentValidationAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/content-validation-agent.ts) is an example of an agent that uses the hook manager to validate entity content against configured rules.

[LLM] The ConstraintSystem component's use of specific modules and components, such as the lib/agent-api/hooks/hook-config.js and integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/content-validation-agent.ts, is a key aspect of its architecture. These modules provide specific functionality, such as loading and merging hook configurations, and validating entity content against configured rules. The system's modular design makes it easy to add or remove modules as needed, which enables developers to customize the system for specific use cases. The GraphDatabaseAdapter (storage/graph-database-adapter.ts) and ViolationCaptureService (scripts/violation-capture-service.js) are other examples of modules that provide specific functionality, such as persistence and violation capture.

[LLM] The system's implementation of lazy initialization for the Large Language Model (LLM) in the ensureLLMInitialized() function (wave-controller.ts:234) is an important design decision. This approach ensures that the LLM is only initialized when it is actually needed, which can help to reduce memory usage and improve system performance. The use of a shared atomic index counter in the runWithConcurrency() function (wave-controller.ts:489) is another important implementation detail. This counter enables the system to efficiently utilize multiple cores and process large amounts of data in parallel. entities.name: SemanticAnalysis
observations: [LLM] The SemanticAnalysis component utilizes a modular architecture, with each agent responsible for a specific task. For instance, the OntologyClassificationAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/ontology-classification-agent.ts) is used for classifying observations against the ontology system. This is evident in the classifyObservations method of the OntologyClassificationAgent class, which takes in a list of observations and returns a list of classified observations. The use of separate modules for different agents and utilities, such as the storage and logging modules, also contributes to the overall modularity of the component. This modular design allows for easier maintenance and updates, as changes to one agent do not affect the others.

[LLM] The SemanticAnalysisAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/semantic-analysis-agent.ts) employs a comprehensive semantic analysis of git and vibe data. This is achieved through the analyzeGitData and analyzeVibeData methods, which process the input data and extract relevant information. The agent also uses the calculateConfidence method of the BaseAgent class to assess the reliability of the analysis results. The confidence calculation system is based on a set of predefined rules and heuristics, which are applied to the analysis results to determine their confidence level. This confidence level is then used to filter out low-confidence results and improve the overall accuracy of the analysis.

[LLM] The CodeGraphAgent (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/code-graph-agent.ts) constructs a knowledge graph of code entities and relationships. This is done by parsing the code and extracting entities such as classes, methods, and variables. The agent then creates a graph data structure to represent the relationships between these entities. The graph is constructed using a set of predefined rules and heuristics, which are applied to the parsed code to determine the relationships between entities. The resulting graph is then used to provide a comprehensive view of the codebase and its structure.

[LLM] The component follows a consistent naming convention, such as PascalCase, throughout the project. This is evident in class names like BaseAgent and ContentValidationAgent, which use PascalCase for their names. This naming convention is also applied to method and variable names, which makes the code easier to read and understand. The use of a consistent naming convention also helps to reduce errors and improves the overall maintainability of the code.

[LLM] The caching mechanism is used to improve performance by reducing the need for repeated computations. This is achieved through the caching configuration options, which allow the component to store the results of expensive computations and reuse them when needed. The caching mechanism is implemented using a combination of in-memory and disk-based caching, which provides a good balance between performance and storage usage. The caching configuration options are defined in the caching configuration file (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/config/caching.ts), which allows for easy customization of the caching behavior.

[LLM] The component's architecture is designed to be highly scalable and flexible. This is achieved through the use of separate modules for different agents and utilities, which allows for easy addition or removal of components as needed. The component also uses a set of predefined interfaces and contracts, which define the expected behavior of each agent and utility. This makes it easy to swap out different implementations of agents and utilities, which improves the overall flexibility and maintainability of the component. The use of a modular architecture also makes it easier to test and debug the component, as each module can be tested and debugged independently.

entities.name: LLMAbstraction
observations: [LLM] The LLMAbstraction component implements a modular design with dependency injection, allowing for easy addition or removal of language models without affecting the overall system. This is evident in the LLMService class (lib/llm/llm-service.ts), which acts as the single public entry point for all LLM operations and handles mode routing, caching, and circuit breaking. The use of a ProviderRegistry to manage different providers, including mock, local, and public providers, further reinforces this modular design.

[LLM] The DMRProvider class (lib/llm/providers/dmr-provider.ts) provides local LLM inference via Docker Desktop's Model Runner, supporting per-agent model overrides from DMR config. This allows for flexibility in model selection and overrides, which can be useful in development and testing environments. The getLLMMode function (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts) also plays a crucial role in determining the LLM mode for a specific agent, prioritizing per-agent overrides, global mode, and agent default from YAML.

[LLM] The AnthropicProvider class (lib/llm/providers/anthropic-provider.ts) uses the Anthropic SDK, which has a different API shape than OpenAI, and handles temperature and system messages differently. This highlights the need for provider-agnostic model calls, which is addressed by the LLMAbstraction component. The LLMService class (lib/llm/llm-service.ts) uses a consistent naming convention and follows a modular architecture, integrating multiple sub-components such as the LLMService, ProviderRegistry, and CircuitBreaker.

[LLM] The LLMState and MockLLMConfig interfaces (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts) define the structure of the LLM state and mock configuration stored in the progress file. The setGlobalLLMMode function (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts) sets the global LLM mode, updating the progress file and legacy mockLLM flag for backward compatibility. This ensures that the component can handle different modes and configurations, and provides a way to manage the state of the LLM.

[LLM] The LLMAbstraction component utilizes a consistent naming convention and follows a modular architecture, integrating multiple sub-components such as the LLMService, ProviderRegistry, and CircuitBreaker. The use of a ProviderRegistry to manage different providers, including mock, local, and public providers, allows for easy addition or removal of language models without affecting the overall system. The getLLMMode function (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts) gets the LLM mode for a specific agent, prioritizing per-agent overrides, global mode, and agent default from YAML, which ensures that the component can handle different modes and configurations.

[LLM] The DMRProvider class (lib/llm/providers/dmr-provider.ts) and AnthropicProvider class (lib/llm/providers/anthropic-provider.ts) demonstrate the ability of the LLMAbstraction component to support multiple providers and handle different API shapes and configurations. The LLMService class (lib/llm/llm-service.ts) acts as the single public entry point for all LLM operations, handling mode routing, caching, and circuit breaking, which provides a unified interface for interacting with different providers. The use of dependency injection to manage budget trackers, sensitivity classifiers, and quota trackers further reinforces the modular design of the component.
